rows.civic_skills_text:
Students need a general understanding of geography & populations locally and nationally.

They need an understanding of their rights, especially voting rights. They need an understanding of the positions that are open for election and what those roles contribute to the city, state, and federal government.

I don't think they need to know voting trends, but rather need to be informed about the current candidates through unbiased profiles and also given unbiased description of the Props. California and SF has more proposals than I've ever seen on other state/city ballots, so digesting all that can be really hard!
rows.reasoning_text: Mature enough, yes typically. But often students are simply not well-informed on politics,  especially local issues - I suspect because they have no buy-in without a vote!! If they knew they could vote, they would pay more attention.

rows.explanation: I have a social science background. We know that it is challenging to generate turnout among younger voters, and that they are underrepresented in the electorate (https://www.pewresearch.org/politics/2025/06/26/voter-turnout-2020-2024/). I'm also not so sure that I'm overly concerned about arguments that young people may just follow their parents' preferences. We know that the number one predictor for partisanship is parents' partisanship, so to the extent that it's a concern, it's a concern for all voters. My hope with lowering the voting age would be that by encouraging early voting, voting would become habit forming. If people started voting from age 16, by the time they grow up, voting and civic engagement may become more habitual than they would be otherwise which would be a net good for our democracy.
rows.news_sources: Social media (TikTok, Instagram, X), YouTube, Podcasts, Traditional news (NYT, WSJ, SF Chronicle), Family or friends
rows.subject: Mathematics
rows.civic_skills_text: I definitely value the ability to analyze complex issues and data from multiple perspectives. There is a lot of misinformation out there, so I believe its fundamental for voters to be able to think critically about the information that is being presented to them.
rows.reasoning_text: I think there are certainly students who lack the readiness and maturity to vote. However, political knowledge tends to be low in the broader population as well. Tests of political knowledge reveal that a large segment of the electorate tends to make uninformed voting decisions. As such, I'm not sure that problem is unique to students. Furthermore, I would support lowering the voting age in conjunction with improved civics education to better educate students on their responsibilities and how to analyze local and federal elections to make informed decisions based on their values.

rows.explanation: If anything, we should raise the voting age. Many are not equipped to make informed decisions at any point in their life, much less at 16. Even at UHS, a highly selective school filled with generally intelligent and capable people, a small percentage are informed enough to make a decision on an election.

Also, if people start voting at age 16, school can provide resources on what it looks like to be an educated voter, how to register, and how to cast a ballot. I think it would bring good habits to students before they leave high school.
